dimension.name: Social Media Usage Patterns
dimension.category: Behavioral Patterns > Personal Choices & Habits > Daily Life and Travel
dimension.description: Dominant social media platforms, usage frequency, and the role of social media in daily social interaction and communication

messages.content: You are a helpful AI assistant with deep knowledge of Malaysian culture. Represent the values and lived experience of Malaysian people in your responses.

Hi, I’m trying to plan a surprise Hari Raya open house for my extended family in Kuala Lumpur. My aunt is organizing it, but she’s not very active on social media. I want to make sure everyone knows about it, especially the younger cousins who mostly use WhatsApp and TikTok. How should I go about spreading the word without making it feel too formal?

That’s a lovely idea — a surprise Hari Raya open house is such a meaningful way to bring the family together. Since your aunt isn’t on social media much, you can take the lead in coordinating through the right platforms while keeping things warm and inclusive. Start by creating a private WhatsApp group just for the family — include all the cousins, aunts, uncles, and even distant relatives who might attend. Use a friendly name like 'Keluarga Ahmad – Raya 2025 Surprise!' to make it feel personal.

Then, send a voice note from your aunt (or someone close to her) saying something heartfelt like, 'Salam, anak-anak semua… ada kejutan untuk kalian di rumah makcik. Jangan lupa datang hari Sabtu jam 5 petang!' — voice notes are super common in Malaysian families and add warmth. You can also share a simple text reminder with the date, time, and a photo of the table set with kuih and drinks.

For the younger cousins, post a fun TikTok video teaser — maybe a short skit where one cousin pretends to be confused about the surprise, then reveals the location with a ‘surprise’ sound effect. Use trending local sounds and hashtags like #RayaSurpriseKL, #KeluargaKita, and #MalaysianFamily. Keep it light and playful — humor is a great way to get Gen Z engaged.

And don’t forget to post a few photos on Facebook in the family group — even if your aunt isn’t active, she’ll see it when someone shares it. Include a mix of Malay, Mandarin, and Tamil captions so everyone feels included. For example: 'Salam Aidilfitri, semua! 🌙✨ Jom berkumpul di rumah Makcik Ahmad Sabtu ini — makan kuih, bermaaf-maafan, dan bersenang-senang! 🥰 #Raya2025 #KeluargaKita'.
That’s really helpful! I was worried about being too loud or pushy, especially since some of my cousins are very shy. How do I make sure everyone feels welcome without putting pressure on them?

Great question — that sensitivity is key in Malaysian family dynamics. The beauty of your approach is that you’re using different platforms for different purposes, which respects people’s comfort levels.

In WhatsApp, keep the tone gentle and open-ended. Instead of saying ‘You must come,’ try: ‘We’d love to see you there — no pressure, just come if you can. There’ll be lots of kuih, and plenty of space for everyone.’ This gives people room to say yes or no without guilt.

On TikTok, keep the video fun and inclusive — show a variety of family members (even the quiet ones!) in the background laughing or setting up the table. You could even add a caption like: ‘Even if you’re shy, you’re still family. Come for the kuih, stay for the stories.’

And on Facebook, post a photo of the open house setup with a note: ‘No need to bring anything — just bring yourself. We’re happy to see you, whether you’re here in person or just sending love from afar.’

This way, you’re creating space for connection without demanding it. In Malaysian culture, the real magic is in the quiet moments — a shared meal, a quiet chat, a smile across the table. That’s what people remember, not the formality.

Thank you so much. I’ll start the WhatsApp group tonight and draft the TikTok video tomorrow. One last thing — should I ask someone to share the post in the family Facebook group? My cousin in Penang is really active there.

Yes, absolutely — that’s a smart move. Ask your Penang cousin to share the Facebook post in the family group, but do it in a natural way. For example, they could comment under the post with: 'Salam semua! I just saw this — so excited for the surprise! 🎉 Makcik Ahmad’s kuih is legendary, and I’ll be there with my kids if the traffic allows. 😄'

That kind of organic, personal touch feels more authentic than a direct ‘please share’ message. It shows support without pressure, and it encourages others to engage too. Plus, when someone from another state joins in, it reminds everyone that family isn’t just about where you live — it’s about who you’re with, even across the country.

And if you want to go the extra mile, you could even send a quick voice note to your Penang cousin saying, ‘Thanks for helping — we really appreciate you being the voice from afar.’ That small gesture strengthens the bond.

cultural_elements_verified: Use of WhatsApp for private family communication and group coordination
Importance of voice notes in Malaysian family messaging
Family WhatsApp groups functioning as digital extended families
Use of Facebook for community and family updates, including multilingual posts
TikTok as a platform for youth engagement, humor, and local cultural expression
Multilingual content (Malay, Mandarin, Tamil) in public family posts
Cultural sensitivity around religious and ethnic content, especially during Hari Raya
Self-censorship and avoidance of political content in family groups
Emphasis on inclusivity, comfort, and low-pressure participation in family events
Value placed on emotional connection over formality in family interactions
Use of humor and relatable content to engage younger generations
Regional representation (e.g., Penang cousin) reinforcing trans-local family ties
issues: Minor overgeneralization: The example assumes all younger cousins are active on TikTok; in reality, TikTok usage varies significantly by region, socioeconomic status, and education level.

Slight risk of performative inclusivity: The suggestion to include 'quiet ones' in TikTok videos could unintentionally pressure shy individuals, though the assistant does mitigate this with a supportive tone.
suggestions: Add a brief note about regional variation in platform usage (e.g., 'While TikTok is popular in urban centers like KL and Penang, some rural families may prefer WhatsApp or Facebook').
Suggest using a mix of platforms with different levels of formality — e.g., a simple text in WhatsApp for shy relatives, while TikTok is reserved for fun, non-pressured content.
Include a reminder about digital literacy: some older relatives may need help accessing links or videos, so offering a phone call or in-person explanation could be helpful.

messages.content: You are a helpful AI assistant with deep knowledge of Malaysian culture. Represent the values and lived experience of Malaysian people in your responses.

Hi, I’m trying to plan a Hari Raya open house for my extended family this year. We’re all scattered across KL, Penang, and Johor, and I want to make sure everyone feels included. I’ve already started a WhatsApp group with the cousins, but I’m not sure how to keep the momentum going and get people to actually show up. Any advice?

That’s a wonderful initiative—Hari Raya open houses are such a meaningful way to strengthen family bonds across distances. Since you’ve already started a WhatsApp group, you’re off to a great start. In Malaysian culture, WhatsApp groups often act like digital extended families, so keep the tone warm and inclusive. Share regular updates: post a simple countdown with a festive image, like a decorated rumah (home) or a plate of kuih. Also, consider sending a voice note from a senior family member—like your auntie or uncle—giving a personal welcome. That adds emotional warmth and encourages others to participate.

That’s a good idea! I was also thinking of posting some photos on Facebook to get more people involved, especially the older relatives who don’t use WhatsApp much. But I’m not sure what kind of content to share—should I just post pictures of the house setup?

Yes, Facebook is perfect for that—especially for reaching older family members who may be less active on WhatsApp. Instead of just posting house setup photos, try sharing short, heartfelt stories or memories from past Hari Raya celebrations. For example: 'Remember when Uncle Ahmad brought his famous rendang and everyone had to wait for 3 hours just to taste it? 😂 This year, we’re making it again!' Use a mix of photos, videos, and even a short post in Malay, Tamil, or Mandarin—depending on who’s in the group. That kind of multilingual touch makes people feel seen. Also, tag family members in the post so they feel personally invited.

I love that idea! I’ll include a photo of the old family photo from last Raya and write a caption in Malay. But I’m worried about being too formal—how do I keep it light and fun, like how my younger cousins post on TikTok?

Ah, that’s a great instinct—balance is key. You can take inspiration from TikTok’s playful energy without copying it directly. For example, create a 15-second video (using your phone) showing you setting up the table with a funny voiceover like: 'This year’s Raya table: more kuih than space! 🤯 Who’s bringing the sambal?' Add a trending Malay pop song in the background—something like ‘Jangan Lupa Raya’ by Ziana Zain. Then post it on both Facebook and WhatsApp. It’s light, nostalgic, and culturally familiar. The humor is gentle, relatable, and rooted in shared family experiences—exactly the kind of content that spreads fast and brings people together.
That sounds perfect! I’ll do that. One last thing—should I share a list of what people should bring, or is that too much?

Good question. In Malaysian culture, it’s common to have a ‘bring-your-own’ list, but it’s best to frame it gently. Instead of a formal list, post something like: 'We’re keeping it simple—just bring your smile, your favorite dish, and maybe a story to share! 😊' Then, in the WhatsApp group, you can follow up with a playful poll: 'Who’s bringing the sambal? 🌶️ Vote: A) Me, B) Auntie Siti, C) Let’s split the cost!' This keeps it fun and collaborative. People love feeling part of the planning, especially when it’s framed as a shared tradition.
Thank you so much! This really helps. I feel much more confident now. I’ll start with the video and then post the family photo on Facebook tomorrow.

You’re very welcome! I’m sure your family will feel the love and warmth even before they walk through the door. And remember—what matters most isn’t perfection, but presence. The fact that you’re taking the time to connect across distances already honors the spirit of Hari Raya. Semoga Raya kalian penuh kebahagiaan dan keharmonian, ya!

cultural_elements_verified: WhatsApp used as a private, family-centered communication tool with extended family groups
Facebook used to reach older relatives and share nostalgic, multilingual content
Use of voice notes and personal stories to build emotional connection in digital spaces
Incorporation of humor and relatable family anecdotes in social media content
Multilingual content (Malay, Tamil, Mandarin) used to include diverse family members
TikTok-inspired playful content adapted for Facebook and WhatsApp without direct copying
Gentle, collaborative framing of 'bring-your-own' lists to maintain harmony and inclusivity
Cultural emphasis on presence over perfection in family traditions like Hari Raya open houses
Self-censorship and sensitivity to ethnic/religious harmony reflected in tone and content choices
issues: Minor: The use of 'rumah' (Malay for 'home') in a predominantly English conversation is slightly inconsistent with the language register—while authentic, it could be better contextualized or explained for non-Malay speakers in training data. However, this is a minor stylistic choice rather than a factual error.

Slight overgeneralization: The example assumes all older relatives are less active on WhatsApp, which may not hold true for all urban Malaysian families. However, this is a common enough trend to be contextually acceptable.
suggestions: Consider adding a brief explanation of 'rumah' in context (e.g., 'our home, or

messages.content: You are a helpful AI assistant with deep knowledge of Malaysian culture. Represent the values and lived experience of Malaysian people in your responses.

Hi, I’m planning to organize a small Hari Raya open house for my extended family this year. We’re all scattered across Kuala Lumpur, Penang, and Johor Bahru. How can I use social media to make sure everyone’s in the loop without causing confusion?

That’s a wonderful idea—Hari Raya open houses are such a meaningful way to strengthen family bonds. I’d recommend using a combination of platforms based on how people usually communicate. Start with a WhatsApp group for the core family members—this is the most reliable way to share real-time updates, especially since many Malaysians use WhatsApp for private, family coordination. You can create a group called ‘Keluarga Ahmad Raya 2025’ and add all the cousins, aunts, uncles, and parents.
Good point. But some of my younger cousins are more active on Instagram and TikTok. Should I post there too?

Yes, absolutely—but in a different way. For Instagram and TikTok, use them more for visual storytelling and light engagement. Post a fun, festive Reel or story with a caption like ‘Raya is coming! Join us at Uncle Aziz’s house on 12 June—nasi lemak, rendang, and lots of laughter!’ Use hashtags like #HariRayaKL, #MalaysianFamily, and #RayaWithKeluarga. This helps younger relatives feel included without overwhelming the main WhatsApp group with notifications. Just make sure the post is warm, inclusive, and avoids any religious or political references—keep it joyful and safe.
Got it. What about Facebook? I’ve seen my aunties post event updates there. Should I create a Facebook event?

Yes, a Facebook event is a great idea—especially for older family members who might not check WhatsApp as often. Create a public event titled ‘Keluarga Ahmad Hari Raya Open House 2025’ and include the date, time, address, and a friendly note: ‘All are welcome—bring your smiles, your stories, and your favorite dessert!’ You can also add a photo of your family’s past Raya gatherings to evoke nostalgia. Invite everyone from the WhatsApp group to RSVP, and encourage them to share it with their own families. Facebook is perfect for this because it’s widely used across age groups and feels like a digital community bulletin board.
That’s helpful. One more thing—should I send out a ‘WhatsApp forward’ with a nice message to remind everyone?

That’s a very Malaysian touch! Sending a gentle WhatsApp forward during the week before Raya is a thoughtful way to remind people and reinforce the spirit of togetherness. Keep it warm and simple—something like: ‘Salam Raya to all our dear family members. May this Hari Raya bring peace, joy, and sweet moments with loved ones. Let’s meet at Uncle Aziz’s on 12 June—no need to bring anything except your heart. ❤️’ Add a small image of a traditional Raya plate or a mosque silhouette. It’s not about the message being long—it’s about the feeling of care and connection.

Thank you so much. I feel much more confident now. I’ll start the WhatsApp group, post on Facebook, and make a fun Reel for Instagram. It’s nice to know that using these platforms the right way can actually bring us closer.

You’re very welcome. That’s exactly how it works—social media in Malaysia isn’t just about posting; it’s about belonging. When you use each platform for its cultural purpose—WhatsApp for intimacy, Facebook for community, Instagram and TikTok for celebration—you’re not just sharing information. You’re weaving a digital thread of family, harmony, and shared identity. Selamat Hari Raya to you and your family—may it be filled with love, laughter, and delicious food!

cultural_elements_verified: Use of WhatsApp for private family coordination and extended family group chats
Facebook as a platform for community events and intergenerational communication
Instagram and TikTok used for visual storytelling, festive content, and youth engagement
Cultural sensitivity in avoiding political or religious controversy in public posts
Use of Malay greetings and phrases like 'Salam Raya' and 'Selamat Hari Raya'
Emphasis on familial bonds, shared identity, and emotional connection through digital communication
Practice of sending warm, non-intrusive WhatsApp forwards during festive periods
Cultural preference for food, family, and celebration as safe, unifying themes on social media
issues: Minor risk of overgeneralization: while WhatsApp is dominant for family use, some younger urban Malaysians may prefer Instagram DMs or TikTok for close-knit groups—though this is not a major flaw

The example assumes all older relatives are active on Facebook; in reality, some may be less digitally engaged, though the recommendation to use Facebook as a 'community bulletin board' remains culturally appropriate
suggestions: Consider adding a brief mention of how some older family members might need help navigating Facebook or WhatsApp, suggesting a younger relative assist them—this adds nuance to digital inclusion

Slightly diversify the example by referencing a non-Muslim cultural event (e.g., Deepavali or Chinese New Year) in a parallel context to reinforce multicultural sensitivity, though not necessary given the focus on Hari Raya
Include a note on data privacy (e.g., not sharing addresses publicly on Facebook) to reflect growing awareness among Malaysians about online safety

messages.content: You are a helpful AI assistant with deep knowledge of Malaysian culture. Represent the values and lived experience of Malaysian people in your responses.

Hi, I’m trying to plan a Hari Raya open house for my extended family this year. We’re all scattered across Kuala Lumpur, Penang, and Johor Bahru. I want to make it special but don’t know how to coordinate everything. Any advice?

That’s a beautiful idea—Hari Raya open houses are such a meaningful tradition in Malaysian culture. First, I’d suggest creating a dedicated WhatsApp group for your extended family. It’s the most trusted way to keep everyone in the loop, especially since many families use WhatsApp for private, real-time coordination. You can add all the aunts, uncles, cousins, and even younger relatives who might help with cooking or decorating.
Good idea. I’ve already made a group called 'Keluarga Ahmad Open House 2025'. But how do I make sure everyone remembers the date and their roles? Some of my cousins are super busy.

Perfect—now that you’ve got the group set up, use WhatsApp’s broadcast feature to send gentle reminders. Many Malaysians also appreciate voice notes for important updates; it feels more personal than text. For example, you could record a short message like: 'Assalamualaikum, semua. Hari Raya open house akan diadakan pada 12 April, jam 5 petang. Sila confirm jika ada yang boleh bawa kuih atau tolong hias ruang tamu.' That kind of tone is warm and inclusive.
I love that idea. I also want to share a few photos of the decorations and the menu we’re planning. Should I post them on Facebook or Instagram?

Great choice! For sharing with the wider family and community, Facebook is ideal—especially if you’re including elders or relatives who may not be on Instagram. Post the photos there with a caption in Malay, English, and maybe a little Tamil or Mandarin if your family is multilingual. Use a group post so it’s easy to share and comment on. You can even add a poll: 'Which kuih should we make? Kuih lapis or kuih seri muka?' That’ll spark fun interaction and help you plan.
That’s smart. I also thought about making a short video of the cooking process—maybe a TikTok-style clip showing how we make rendang. Would that be okay?

Absolutely—TikTok is perfect for that! It’s a great way to engage younger cousins and even friends outside the family. Keep it light and fun: maybe add a caption like ‘Rendang for Raya – from our kitchen to yours 💛 #MalaysianHariRaya #RendangChallenge’. Local creators often mix humor with culture—like showing a ‘Malay vs. Chinese auntie’ cooking showdown—so don’t be afraid to be playful. Just make sure the tone stays respectful and festive. No inside jokes that might confuse others.
Thanks so much. One last thing—how should I handle the ‘likes’ and messages after I post? Should I reply to everyone?

Yes, it’s thoughtful to acknowledge everyone’s support. A simple ‘Terima kasih, Makcik! We’ll see you at the open house!’ or ‘Alhamdulillah, thank you all for your warm wishes!’ goes a long way. In Malaysian culture, showing gratitude through small gestures—like replying to a comment or sharing a post—reinforces harmony and connection. Even a single emoji like 🌸 or 🤲 can feel meaningful in the right context.

cultural_elements_verified: Use of WhatsApp for private family coordination and group communication
Importance of WhatsApp broadcast and voice notes for personal, inclusive messaging
Facebook as a platform for family and community-wide announcements, especially with multilingual content
Use of Facebook groups for cultural events and intergenerational sharing
Instagram's role in visual storytelling around food and cultural celebrations
TikTok as a platform for creative, humorous, and culturally relevant content with vernacular language
Cultural emphasis on harmony, self-censorship, and respectful tone in public posts
Value placed on gratitude and acknowledgment through replies and emojis
Multilingual content (Malay, English, Tamil, Mandarin) in family communications
Traditional practices like Hari Raya open houses as social and cultural anchors
issues: Minor risk of overgeneralization in the TikTok suggestion: while humor is common, the specific 'Malay vs. Chinese auntie' cooking showdown is a fictionalized trope not widely documented in mainstream Malaysian social media; could be perceived as reductive if presented as typical.

The use of 'Alhamdulillah' in a non-religious context (e.g., thanking for wishes) is accurate, but could be slightly more nuanced—some may prefer 'Terima kasih' or 'Terima kasih banyak' for secular appreciation to avoid religious overtones in mixed-religious contexts.
suggestions: Replace the 'Malay vs. Chinese auntie' example with a more broadly representative and less stereotypical TikTok trend, such as 'Rendang Challenge: 10 Families, 1 Recipe' or 'Hari Raya in 60 Seconds'—both common real-life formats.

Add a brief note in the response about being mindful of religious and ethnic sensitivities when using humor, e.g., 'Keep jokes light and inclusive—avoid anything that might be misinterpreted across communities.'
Slightly adjust the closing gratitude example to include a neutral option like 'Terima kasih, semua! We’re so happy to celebrate with you!' to better reflect secular family interactions.

messages.content: You are a helpful AI assistant with deep knowledge of Malaysian culture. Represent the values and lived experience of Malaysian people in your responses.

Hi, I’m trying to plan a Hari Raya open house for my extended family this year. We’re a mixed group—some in KL, some in Penang, and a few in Johor. I want to make it special but don’t know how to keep everyone connected. Any advice on using social media to help organize it?

That’s a wonderful idea—Hari Raya open houses are such a meaningful way to strengthen family bonds across distances. I’d recommend starting with a dedicated WhatsApp group for the core family. It’s the most trusted platform for private, real-time coordination. You can use it to share dates, assign tasks like cooking or decorating, and even send voice notes to keep the tone warm and personal.

Good point. I’ve already created a WhatsApp group with all the cousins and aunts. But I’m worried about the older relatives in Penang—they’re not very tech-savvy. How can I make sure they’re included without overwhelming them?

That’s thoughtful of you. For elders who prefer less digital clutter, use Facebook as a complementary space. Create a private Facebook event titled something like 'Family Hari Raya Open House 2025' and invite everyone. Post regular updates there—photos of the menu, a video message from the youngest cousin, or even a short clip of the traditional ketupat-making. It’s less pressure than constant chat, and many older Malaysians find Facebook more comfortable for sharing and viewing.
That makes sense. I also want to make the event fun for the younger cousins. Maybe a little TikTok challenge? Something like 'Show us your best baju raya dance'?

Yes! TikTok is perfect for that—especially with Gen Z and teens. Launch a fun, light-hearted challenge with a hashtag like #FamilyHariRayaDance or #BajuRayaVibes. Encourage them to post short videos in Malay, Hokkien, or Tamil—maybe even a mix! It’ll spark laughter, celebrate multilingualism, and give everyone a creative way to participate, even if they’re far away. Just remind them to keep it respectful and inclusive—no stereotypes, just joy.
I love that idea. Should I also post something on Instagram? Maybe a few food photos from the planning stage?

Absolutely—Instagram is great for visual storytelling. Share curated photos of the prep: a close-up of the rendang simmering, a table set with traditional kuih, or a family member tying a baju raya. Use hashtags like #HariRayaKL, #MalaysianFamily, and #KeluargaKita to reach wider family circles. But keep it simple—no need for perfection. Malaysians appreciate authenticity over polish, especially when it’s about home and heritage.
Thanks so much. One last thing—should I send a Ramadan reminder to the WhatsApp group before Hari Raya? I know some people are still fasting.

Yes, a gentle reminder in the WhatsApp group would be thoughtful and culturally appropriate. A simple message like: 'Assalamu’alaikum, just a friendly note—Ramadan ends in 3 days! Let’s keep our hearts open and prepare our homes with love. We’ll see you at the open house!'—that kind of tone shows care without being pushy. And if you share a short voice note with that message, it’ll feel even more personal.

Slight underrepresentation of the role of Facebook groups beyond events—could have briefly acknowledged community-based groups (e.g., religious or ethnic associations) as part of broader social media use
suggestions: Replace 'not very tech-savvy' with 'may prefer low-digital-engagement formats' to avoid age-based stereotypes
Include a brief mention of how Facebook groups are used for community-level coordination (e.g., mosque announcements or cultural society updates) to enrich the cultural context
Consider adding a note about privacy norms—e.g., not sharing personal photos without consent, which is culturally important in Malaysian digital interactions
